title: Multi-subject identity edits: chained single-ref passes (two people in one pass is a model limit, not a bug)
description:
Putting TWO people through a krea2 identity edit in a single pass loses facial identity for both. Extensively reproduced 2026-07-19. This is a documented limitation of the LoRA, NOT a defect in our graph or our prompting — and no knob we currently expose fixes it.

EVIDENCE THAT IT IS A MODEL LIMIT (all user-run, 2026-07-19):
- Both subjects drift, including the FAVOURABLE one: a large, well-lit, high-detail reference occupying much of the frame lost identity just as badly as a small background face. If reference quality were the binding constraint, those two would not fail equally.
- Clothing survives while faces do not — matching the model card's own description: 'Likeness is texture-faithful, proportion-conservative... strongly distinctive facial geometry regresses toward typical proportions.' Texture survives, geometry regresses.
- LoRA v1.1 -> v1.2 barely moved it, despite v1.2's headline being 'better face likeness'. A version bump not moving the needle points away from the LoRA being the binding constraint in this configuration.
- Single-reference edits work well on the same setup — user has been dropping the second image for exactly this reason.
- The model card names the failure and the fix directly: two-subject edits 'faces drift toward each other'; the documented workaround is to 'chain single-ref inserts (place person A, then a second edit pass adding person B from their reference)'.

RULED OUT (do not re-test these):
- Slot wiring / input order. Traced and confirmed correct: scene -> Input_Image -> image/source_latent, subject -> Input_Image_2 -> image_b/source_latent_b, matching the node's trained order. See MPI-312.
- Reference downscaling. User tested pre-downscaling image_b: destroyed shirt text and clothing texture while the face deviated EXACTLY as much. Face identity does not ride the pixel/latent path, so starving or feeding that path does not move it.
- Generating at lower megapixels. Tested previously; generations break above ~1.5MP and the face did not track resolution.
- Prompt phrasing. Worth improving independently (the card's examples are referential — 'this man', 'this elven woman and her goblin best friend' — while the failing prompt said 'the two women' with no per-subject anchor), but two independent enhancer-side attempts already failed and the layer is wrong, not the wording.

WHAT THIS CARD WOULD BUILD:
A chained multi-pass edit: pass 1 places subject A into the scene, pass 2 takes that OUTPUT as the new scene and adds subject B from their own reference. Each pass stays in the configuration that demonstrably works (one scene + one subject, trained order).

OPEN QUESTIONS (none of these are settled — this is an idea, not a plan):
- Is this an App (App Library outcome app, multi-step pipeline) or a mode on the existing edit op? An app is the closer precedent for multi-pass work, but the trigger is arguably just 'a second person chip'.
- How is per-subject prompt intent expressed? Each pass needs its own delta instruction ('add this woman on the right'), which the current single prompt box does not model.
- Does pass 2 degrade pass 1's subject? Untested. The edit path can alter regions outside the intended edit (model card: 'Add/remove/replace operations can sometimes alter other parts of the frame or shift color grade'), so subject A's face may drift during pass 2. THIS IS THE KEY RISK — if it does, chaining does not solve the problem either and the feature is not worth building. Test this manually BEFORE any code.
- Cost: two full generations per result, plus the intermediate. Acceptable for an app-style outcome, worse as an invisible default.

FIRST STEP IS A MANUAL TEST, NOT CODE: run the chain by hand in ComfyUI (edit A into scene, save, feed as scene, edit B in) and check whether A's identity survives pass 2. That single result decides whether this card is worth planning.

events.note: Surfaced while validating krea2 identity-edit v1.2. Two-subject single-pass identity loss reproduced repeatedly and traced to a documented LoRA limitation rather than our wiring or prompting; upstream prescribes chained single-ref passes. Carded as an idea — gated on a manual chain test proving subject A survives pass 2.

attention.reason: Idea stage, deliberately unplanned. Blocked on one manual test: does pass 2 preserve pass 1's subject? If not, chaining is not the answer either.
